segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a time-lapse of the bustling Mall of America, showcasing crowds moving across multiple levels with escalators and storefronts visible, all set to a light, investigative background music. A male voice (Voice 1) states, "The current credit scoring system, as it stands, is flawed," as the scene transitions to a close-up of a person in a yellow shirt inserting a credit card into a payment terminal. Another male voice (Voice 2) then explains, "And this error of being fundamentally misaligned has huge ramifications across the system for millions of Americans," while a man in a gray suit speaks directly to the camera against a plain background. As a man in a black suit walks through a crowded city street, a third male voice (Voice 3) declares, "There's no doubt about this. Visa is dominant." The narrative continues with a close-up of a hand swiping a credit card at a point-of-sale terminal, followed by a person holding a child inserting a card at a grocery store checkout, with fruit in the background. During these scenes, Voice 1 returns to discuss credit cards, mentioning "Amex card or a Chase card" and that "Discover really is kind of for the masses." A man in a dark suit and glasses then speaks to the camera in a well-lit room, with a fourth male voice (Voice 4) asserting, "There's no reason to think that American Express won't have a strong customer base after the next recession." An aerial view of a city neighborhood appears, overlaid with the CNBC logo and the title 'How credit cards work in the U.S.', as a female voice (Voice 5) announces, "6.7 trillion dollars." This figure is visually reinforced by a money-counting machine processing cash, displaying '$6.7 trillion' on screen, with Voice 5 continuing, "That is how much Americans spent using their debit or credit cards in 2019." The segment concludes with a busy shopping street and a graphic stating 'More than 60% of those purchases were made using Visa,' followed by a close-up of a Chase Visa card and a montage of various Visa cards over a dollar bill, as Voice 5 explains, "More than 60% of those purchases were made using cards from Visa, a company that has long dominated the payment card industry." The background music then fades out.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a Visa card being tapped on a payment terminal, as Doug Kantor, Executive Committee Member of the Merchants Payments Coalition, highlights that the majority of US payment card transactions are on Visa cards. Kantor, seen in a black suit and red patterned tie, then discusses the extensive litigation Visa has faced, including from the Department of Justice, an exterior shot of which is shown, followed by a close-up of the FBI seal. A payment terminal with a hand inserting a card is overlaid with the text 'Market Power,' visually representing Kantor's assertion that legal decisions confirm Visa's "market power" as a matter of law, concluding that Visa is dominant. A green Visa card is then swiped, and a cashier processes a payment at a 'TOP IT!' counter, as Lisa Ellis, Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson LLC, begins to speak. A graphic from The Economist, "How Visa Became the Top Dog in Global Finance," appears, followed by a payment terminal displaying '$480 billion valuation,' aligning with Ellis's statement that Visa was valued at over $480 billion as of October 2021, making it one of America's most valuable companies. A graph comparing Visa Inc.'s performance to the S&P 500 highlights a '>170% gain in the past 5 years,' as Ellis details Visa's $21.8 billion net revenue for 2020 and its significant stock growth. A montage of various Visa cards, including a Wells Fargo Visa Debit card, reinforces the brand's widespread presence. The segment concludes with Ellis, seen in a home setting with a fireplace, wearing a dark floral blouse, explaining how to conceptualize Visa's revenue stream based on every $100 spent on a Visa card.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with Lisa Ellis, Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson LLC, speaking from a home setting, explaining in her clear voice that Visa makes "about a quarter, meaning 25 cents" on every $100 shoe purchase. As she speaks, a hand places a black shoe into a box, and another presses buttons on a payment terminal. Ellis continues, noting that as the network scales, "very high incremental margins" lead to increased profitability. A magnifying glass highlights numbers like '7,465.95' and '125.25' on a receipt, followed by a blurred shot of cash being handled at a counter. A female narrator then states that Visa's success hasn't always been good for merchants, as a tablet displays a Kingsport Times News headline about swipe fees being hidden costs. Jared Scheeler, CEO of The Hub Convenience Stores, Inc., appears, expressing in his concerned voice his desire for "relief in the swipe fee arena," criticizing the "way too much" money Visa makes and the "lack of competition" among issuing banks. His words accompany a close-up of a payment terminal with Visa and MasterCard logos and a hand swiping a card. A Yahoo Finance headline about swipe fees threatening small businesses is overlaid on a person holding a receipt and phone. Scheeler concludes by calling the situation "really unfair." The female narrator returns, asking how Visa makes money and why it dominates payments, as a close-up of a Visa card and then a blurred montage of credit card logos (Visa, MasterCard, Discover) are shown with the text 'How Visa makes money'.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with black-and-white archival footage of a bustling city street in front of a Bank of America building, with pedestrians and vintage cars, as a female narrator explains that 'The Bank of America launched Visa as the nation's first licensed credit card for middle-class consumers and small to medium-sized merchants in 1958.' Close-ups of the bank’s 'TEXTILE BRANCH' and 'UNITED STATES DEPOSITARY' signage emphasize its institutional presence. An archival male voiceover states, 'The computer is changing our world. The way we do business,' as the scene transitions to early credit card processing, showing a hand swiping a card through a bulky terminal. A cashier at a retail checkout uses a 'BARCLAYCARD VISA' reader, coinciding with an archival female voiceover (with a British accent) noting, 'Every month, British shoppers signed for one and a half billion pounds worth of goods on a credit card.' The footage includes a montage of early point-of-sale systems and data entry screens, highlighting the evolution of payment technology, while the narrator continues, 'By 1970, Bank of America gave up its direct control over the card... The company grew quickly after, expanding internationally by 1974, and introducing its first debit card in 1975.' A brief sound of an old computer processing is heard. A modern office setting shows professionals reviewing a 'Sale Report' with charts, followed by a shot of the New York Stock Exchange with a Reuters headline overlay: 'Visa Raises $17.9 billion in Record IPO' dated March 18, 2008, as the narrator details, 'In 2007, Visa completed its corporate restructuring with the formation of Visa Inc. and went public in 2008, raising $17.9 billion in one of the largest US public offerings to date.' The segment concludes with Doug Kantor, a man in a black suit and red patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera, stating, 'Visa was set up to be dominant.' Throughout, a consistent background music track plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Doug Kantor, a man in a black suit and red patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a professional indoor setting. His clear, authoritative male voice explains that Visa "started actually as an association of thousands of banks across the country," which "came together and established Visa to have this national credit card," gaining a "dominant position because it was virtually all the banks." The scene then transitions to Lisa Ellis, a Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson LLC, who, from a home environment with a fireplace and family photo, states in a clear female voice that "there are still Class B shares of Visa stock which are actually owned still by those original banks." A professional female narrator then takes over, accompanied by light, corporate background music, as archival footage of an early Docutel ATM shows a hand inserting a card, prompting 'PLEASE ENTER YOUR PERSONAL IDENTIFICATION NUMBER,' followed by keypad interaction for 'CASH FROM CHECKING.' The narrator details how "Today, Visa has grown to become one of the world's largest payment processing networks," with "over 3.4 billion cards in the market across over 200 countries and territories." The visuals shift to an exterior shot of JPMorgan Chase & Co. at Two Seventy Park Avenue, then a top-down view of a woman using a modern outdoor ATM labeled 'BANK CREDIT GROUP.' A close-up of a hand swiping a card is overlaid with text '3.4 billion Visa cards in the market,' as the narrator reveals Visa "generated over 4 trillion dollars in purchase volume in the United States, according to the February 2021 Nielsen report." A silhouette of a person using a tablet in front of a large digital screen displaying financial data is shown, followed by a time-lapse of people walking with shopping bags, overlaid with text '$4.17 trillion in purchase volume in the U.S. during 2020.' The narrator then compares, "In comparison, Mastercard has over 2.3 billion cards in the market with a purchase volume of roughly 1.7 trillion," as a close-up of a Mastercard appears, displaying text comparing Visa and Mastercard's card numbers and U.S. purchase volumes. The segment concludes with a close-up of a chip card, and Doug Kantor returns to the screen, his voice reinforcing that "Visa does have a huge number of cards out there, more than Mastercard without question," as the background music fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a retail checkout where a customer hands a red wallet to a cashier processing payment on a terminal. On-screen text highlights the '2% to 4% profit margin range in retail industry,' as a male voice, later identified as Doug Kantor, articulates that profit margins tend to be in the "two, three, maybe four percent range." The scene transitions to a person using a tablet to browse products while holding a credit card, suggesting online shopping. A female voice, identified as Lisa Ellis, then explains that "as a business, the reason it's so profitable is because it's a primarily fixed cost business," noting that once the infrastructure is in place, "each incremental transaction... comes in at extremely high incremental margins" due to a "massive capacity network." The female narrator elaborates on the four entities in the model: the customer, the bank, the merchant, and Visa as the connector. The visuals show a person using a smartphone and credit card, likely for online shopping, then transition to a retail checkout scene where a customer inserts a card into a terminal, with the 'Four-Party Model' text overlay listing the key players. A second female voice then describes Visa as a 'physical network,' akin to a telecom or internet network, but one that specifically 'carries money.' segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Lisa Ellis, a senior equity analyst at MoffettNathanson LLC, speaking from her home, which features a fireplace mantel adorned with a silver platter, blue candles, and a framed family photo. Gesturing expressively, she explains the fundamental trust in the Visa ecosystem, allowing consumers to use a Visa-branded piece of plastic at any merchant globally for a successful transaction, emphasizing that without the Visa brand, the card wouldn't work. As she speaks, the scene transitions to a large outdoor billboard in Vietnam promoting Visa alongside MasterCard and Cirrus, featuring a woman in a traditional conical hat, with a Coca-Cola sign below. This is followed by a red promotional poster in China highlighting Visa’s partnership with China Merchants Bank, offering discounts at brands like DKNY and Tommy Hilfiger, complete with a cartoon cat and QR code. A time-lapse then shows people moving through the Mall of America, with escalators and storefronts visible, before cutting to a close-up of a Visa-branded poster from the Torino 2006 Olympics featuring a Chinese speed skater. A payment terminal displaying 'DECLINED' over a stack of U.S. currency and coins visually underscores transaction failure. A clear female narrator then states that international transaction revenues take up about 22% of the company's gross revenue, while a pie chart illustrates Visa’s 2020 gross revenue breakdown, showing 39% from international transaction revenues, 34% from service revenues, 22% from data processing fees, and 5% from other revenues. An aerial view of a dense urban skyline, likely New York City, precedes Lisa Ellis's return to commentary. She discusses Visa's continuous investment in other payment types for future revenue, such as B2B payments, disbursements like those for Uber or Lyft drivers, insurance payouts, and person-to-person payments. During her explanation, a CNBC article title about Visa's fintech ventures appears, followed by a hand holding a smartphone with a ride-hailing app interface indicating 'DRIVER EN ROUTE,' a masked driver in a car wearing gloves, and a person using a laptop on a bed. The segment concludes with an exterior shot of the Visa headquarters, partially obscured by green foliage.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a time-lapse shot of a modern, multi-level office building, displaying the text 'Relationship with merchants,' as a female narrator states that payment will be an increasing part of their business. Doug Kantor, Executive Committee Member of the Merchants Payments Coalition, then appears, speaking directly to the camera in a black suit and red tie, while the narrator explains Visa's history of legal cases and investigations. A close-up of a wooden gavel striking a sound block visually emphasizes legal action as Doug Kantor, in a clear, authoritative male voice, begins to detail how the Department of Justice has sued Visa multiple times and entered consent decrees. As a hand inserts a card into an ATM, with a green light indicating acceptance, Doug Kantor elaborates on Visa's past rules preventing banks from issuing competitor cards like Discover or American Express, which was deemed an antitrust problem. An exterior shot of a classical government building with an American flag waving reinforces the legal context. Doug Kantor reappears, continuing to explain Visa's rules tying credit and debit card acceptance. The scene then shows hands holding a credit card and using a smartphone, with gift boxes in the background, as Doug Kantor concludes that few industries have faced more antitrust litigation. A PR Newswire text overlay, announcing a '$5.54-6.24 Billion Settlement Providing Payments to Merchants Who Accepted Visa or Mastercard since 2004' dated Feb. 22, 2019, appears over an aerial city view, visually confirming the narrator's subsequent announcement of Visa and Mastercard agreeing to a $5.5 billion settlement for excessive merchant fees. The segment concludes with a hand inserting a card into a payment terminal at a retail checkout, as the narrator refers to this as the largest ever class action.

This transitions to a CNBC news graphic announcing 'Visa abandons takeover of Plaid after DOJ raises antitrust concerns,' dated January 12, 2021, accompanied by a close-up of a gavel striking a sound block, symbolizing legal action. The narrator explains Visa's abandonment of its $5.3 billion takeover of Plaid. The scene shifts to a television screen showing a female news anchor delivering breaking news, stating that 'Visa and Plaid are terminating their $5 billion merger,' with the headline 'VISA, PLAID CALL OFF MERGER' displayed. The video then cuts to the exterior of the Department of Justice building, emphasizing the government's role, as the narrator explains the DOJ filed an antitrust lawsuit to limit competition. A silhouette of Lady Justice holding scales and a sword is then overlaid with a Bloomberg headline about Visa and Mastercard ATM access fee lawsuits receiving class certification, while the narrator details a federal judge certifying a class action lawsuit against Visa and Mastercard for excessive ATM fees. The narrative continues with a close-up of hands using an ATM, entering a PIN, and retrieving a card, with the text 'Visa declined to comment' appearing, as the narrator confirms Visa's refusal to comment. This is followed by a cashier processing a payment at a retail checkout, then a close-up of a payment terminal with a card being swiped, as the narrator highlights retailers' arguments that Visa's swipe fees are too high for smaller businesses. The video then features Jared Scheeler, CEO of The Hub Convenience Stores, Inc., speaking directly to the camera in front of a bookshelf, stating that consumers don't think about swipe fees, but business owners certainly do, as for him, swipe fees are the second highest expense line item on his P&L, right after labor. The final scenes show a low-angle view of a convenience store aisle with shoppers walking by, reinforcing the retail context of the payment systems discussed.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a payment terminal, its keypad and buttons highlighted, as a calm, informative female narrator details the escalating costs of card payments. Text overlays sequentially reveal that Visa and Mastercard collected $25.6 billion in swipe fees in 2009, which more than doubled to $67.6 billion by 2019. The narrator continues, stating that overall processing fees for all card payments reached $110 billion in 2020, citing the National Retail Federation and HSN Consultants/Nilson Report. A hand is then seen inserting a card into a terminal, visually reinforcing the payment process. Doug Kantor, an Executive Committee Member of the Merchants Payments Coalition, appears in a professional indoor setting, speaking directly to the camera, explaining in a clear male voice that these fees tend to be around '10% of what merchants pay on a transaction.' The scene shifts to Jared Scheeler, CEO of The Hub Convenience Stores, Inc., speaking from his home office, who passionately expresses his frustration in a distinct male voice, stating, 'it saddens me because so many people have come to accept it as it is what it is,' and emphasizing, 'these prices are so ridiculous... we have to do something about it.' The video then cuts to a person holding a smartphone and a credit card, preparing for an online payment, while Kantor's voice returns, further elaborating that 'this is the banks acting collectively and setting price,' highlighting the core issue of market dominance.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with Doug Kantor, Executive Committee Member of the Merchants Payments Coalition, speaking directly to the camera in a professional indoor setting, wearing a black suit with a red patterned tie, asserting in a clear male voice that businesses should be competing on price like all other American businesses do. A female narrator then explains that supporters argue Visa stands with merchants, not banks. The visual transitions to a close-up of a hand scanning a product with a smartphone at a grocery checkout, with oranges in the background, followed by a woman with short white hair and red glasses, wearing an apron, interacting with a payment terminal. Lisa Ellis, Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson LLC, appears from her home setting with a fireplace and family photo, wearing a dark floral blouse and gesturing. Her articulate female voice explains that Visa's business structure is balanced and even skewed toward merchants, who get the majority of their revenue from banks and the supporting ecosystem. As an exterior shot of a Chase bank branch on a city street and a close-up of a hand inserting a credit card into a payment terminal are shown, Ellis continues, describing Visa as a pretty agnostic, central party facilitating effective digital payments, balancing both sides. The narrator returns, stating that Visa has effectively changed the world of commerce forever. The video then shows two women in a retail store looking at a tablet and laptop, and a woman in a brown blazer holding a red credit card and smartphone at a desk. Ellis's voice resumes, calling Visa a victim of its own success, so ubiquitous, secure, and easy to use that people begin to take it for granted. The segment concludes with a man in a checkered shirt on a brown leather couch holding a credit card and talking on the phone, while Lisa Ellis, seen again with animated expressions and gestures, describes Visa as a fantastic enabler of their life.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a customer handing cash to a cashier at a grocery store, as Lisa Ellis, Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson LLC, begins speaking in a clear female voice about the past necessity of cash or checks. The scene transitions to Ellis, speaking from her home, gesturing expressively as she explains that cash is expensive for merchants, requiring cash drawers, armored trucks, and managerial staff to count and secure it. This is visually reinforced by close-ups of hands counting and organizing U.S. currency in a cash register drawer, followed by a hand placing a $100 bill on a stack. Ellis then shifts to discussing the alternatives to cash, noting that while card payments have their own costs, modern contactless payments, like a customer using a smartphone at a restaurant, are quick, easy, and speed up checkout lines, facilitating commerce. A quick montage of various credit cards, including a black chip card, underscores these digital options. A distinct, slightly suspenseful electronic music track begins as a CNBC graphic appears, asking 'Why wealthy Americans love AmEx,' accompanied by luxury images. A male voice then states that Amex made $52.9 billion in total revenues net of interest expense in 2022. The segment concludes with a bar chart showing 'Results for U.S. card networks at midyear (2022 purchase transactions),' with Visa leading, followed by Mastercard, American Express, and Discover, as the male voice continues to discuss Amex's impressive earnings before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a close-up of a gold American Express card, its intricate design and Centurion logo highlighted, as a man in a dark suit and glasses, speaking in a clear, informative tone, explains how American Express has had multiple products like the gold and platinum cards, focusing on both consumer and corporate card business. The scene transitions to a blue animated graphic illustrating the American Express closed-loop system, showing a circular flow between cardholder and merchant with Amex at the center. The man in the dark suit continues, explaining that what sets Amex apart is its network operation, contrasting it with an open-loop system diagram featuring Visa and Mastercard, where cardholder and merchant banks are also involved, as he describes how most credit cards from companies like Visa and Mastercard function in an open-loop system. The video then cuts to a man in a black suit and blue tie, speaking against a solid green background and gesturing, who elaborates on the open-loop system, detailing how a cardholder using their card generates revenue by relaying information from the issuing banks to the acquiring banks or the merchant's bank. He then explains that Amex, on the other hand, operates in a closed-loop system, functioning as the issuer, the acquirer, and the network combined. A close-up of a payment terminal shows a Mastercard being swiped, before cutting back to the man in the black suit who continues his explanation, emphasizing that Amex is different from Visa and Mastercard because Amex is a lender, whereas Visa and Mastercard are merely card networks that process transactions but don't actually issue credit. He concludes by stating that American Express is both a lender of credit and a card network. The segment finishes with a shot of a sign displaying multiple card logos, including American Express, Visa, Mastercard, and Discover, reinforcing the variety of payment networks in use.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with the Man in Black Suit, wearing a blue patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera against a solid green background, gesturing as he explains the concept of a "processor of transactions." The scene then transitions to a retail checkout where a customer in a checkered shirt hands a card to a cashier, who processes the payment on a tablet, with the customer signing a digital receipt displaying a total of $79.18. Concurrently, the Man in Dark Suit and Glasses, in a well-lit room with a window, elaborates on how this system "enables them to see exactly what their customers are spending down to the item, and have all that extra data where they can then advertise or target different reward spends across that," contrasting it as "very different than what Visa and Mastercard can see, which would be just really total dollar amounts." Doug Kantor, an Executive Committee Member of the Merchant Payments Coalition, then appears in a professional indoor setting with a headset, explaining that it "allows them to tailor some of those deals, especially on the merchant side, if there's a reason why they want a specific merchant's business, they can change their normal terms in order to fit that situation," adding that "they don't have to worry about a bank being upset about what those terms are, whereas Visa and Mastercard would." During this, a close-up shows an American Express sign on a glass door, followed by a promotional poster for 10% off concessions at Brooklyn Steel when using an AmEx card, and an aerial view of a city skyline featuring the Bank of America and Wachovia buildings. The Man in Dark Suit and Glasses reappears, stating, "This closed-loop system also allows Amex to earn money from interest, unlike Visa and Mastercard." Visually, a couple reviews financial documents and credit cards at a desk, followed by a graphic overlay highlighting '$9.9 billion AmEx’s net interest income in 2022' over a blurred statement of account, as he concludes, "The company generated about $9.9 billion in net interest income in 2022." The segment finishes with the Man in Black Suit reappearing against the green background, continuing his explanation with expressive hand gestures, noting it's "advantageous to be diver..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Lisa Ellis, Partner and Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson, speaking directly to the camera from a home setting. She wears a dark sleeveless top and a pearl necklace, gesturing expressively as she articulates how companies create personalized rewards that "feel to you as the consumer like this program was custom designed for me." She explains their ability to recruit top hotels and restaurants for specialized offers, bringing affluent consumers to them, and how "everyone sees benefits from their role in the middle of connecting those dots." As she speaks, the scene briefly cuts to a modern hotel lobby where a man in a dark suit interacts with a receptionist, a large screen displaying 'CARPENTER SERVICES' in the background, before returning to Lisa, who continues her animated discussion. The visuals then transition to a luxurious outdoor poolside celebration with a server carrying champagne to laughing guests, followed by a dimly lit, upscale bar where a bartender serves smiling patrons. A close-up shows a customer making a contactless payment with a smartphone at a restaurant table. A male voice then takes over, explaining that "having an affluent customer base also gives the bonus advantage of decreased credit risk," noting that "delinquency rates for Amex have remained substantially lower compared to other major issuing banks." This commentary likely aligns with the visual of a man in a dark suit and glasses speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit room. Another male voice then adds that "credit losses through the cycle will move really closely with unemployment as you would expect," and that "changes in unemployment, they roughly go up between 1.7 times and 2.25 times." The final scenes depict economic hardship, showing protesters outdoors holding signs like '22,000,000 UNEMPLOYED' and 'AMERICA NEEDS GOOD JOBS,' followed by a storefront with large red 'STORE CLOSING' banners advertising 'EVERYTHING $3 OR LESS.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a vibrant poolside 'SWIM & STYLE' event presented by ZYNC from American Express, featuring guests lounging and a table adorned with an American Express card display and floral arrangements. A man in a black suit with a blue patterned tie, speaking against a solid green background, explains that Amex is trying to reach a younger audience that will be the leaders and heavy spenders of tomorrow through experiences like travel, dining, or exclusive concerts, referencing a lively concert scene with a crowd dancing under purple lights and a group of friends taking a selfie in front of a 'LOUISVILLE BY JACK HARLOW' backdrop sponsored by American Express. He continues, stating Amex has also made meaningful investments in scaling and improving its technology to be more competitive against alternative premium cards, as a customer is seen using a smartphone for a contactless payment at a modern retail store checkout, with a cashier scanning items and handing over a shopping bag. The man in the black suit then discusses Amex's progress abroad, noting they were the first US-based credit card issuer to win approval in China and are partnering with local brands to tap into that increasingly affluent consumer audience, coinciding with a view of an American Express promotional kiosk in a shopping mall displaying card details and Chinese text advertising the Explorer card. Following this, a close-up shows a hand placing 10 Euro banknotes into a cash register drawer. Lisa Ellis, Partner and Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson, speaking from a home setting with company logos behind her, then provides an analytical perspective, stating that credit card adoption in Europe (like France and Germany) is much lower than in the US, the UK, and Australia, where it's quite high, thus presenting a huge growth opportunity. The segment concludes with the man in the black suit reappearing, continuing his explanation with expressive hand gestures, suggesting that Amex is increasingly operating like a tech company.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Black Suit, dressed in a black suit and blue patterned tie against a green background, gesturing as he explains concepts like 'web experiences' and 'data gathering.' He continues, discussing how 'buy now pay later' could be a 'big threat to the Amex model,' as the scene transitions to an outdoor urban street where a digital sign prominently displays 'Klarna available. Ask to pay with Klarna at checkout,' with pedestrians passing by. The Man in Black Suit returns, gesturing as he explains that 'they were the first traditional credit card issuer to unveil their version,' leading into a close-up of the Person in Yellow Shirt, wearing a yellow long-sleeve shirt and smartwatch, holding a smartphone and a black credit card, preparing for an online purchase with 'Amex Pay It Plan It' text overlaid. He elaborates, stating that 'a few years ago they came out with Amex Pay It Planet, which I think again speaks to offering something for everybody.' The scene shifts to hands sorting through various credit cards, including a gold Citi and a Visa, before cutting to Doug Kantor, Executive Committee Member of the Merchant Payments Coalition, speaking in a professional indoor setting with a headset, stating that 'the biggest threat for Amex is the competition within the credit card industry.' Doug Kantor continues, elaborating that 'the biggest weakness or danger for American Express really is that marketplace power of Visa and Mastercard and what they may decide to do with it, which may or may not be anything that American Express can control.' A blue banner on a sunny street pole, reading 'Ready to ACCEPT VISA' with a Visa card image and a woman in a conical hat walking below, is shown before Lisa Ellis, Partner and Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson, appears in a home setting with company logos, gesturing as she discusses how 'the value that they are able to offer from the closed loop model is distinctive and unique,' and how 'data analytics and AI get better.' The segment concludes with a close-up of a woman wearing glasses, her reflection showing data, followed by multiple credit cards scattered on a wooden surface.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Lisa Ellis, Partner and Senior Equity Analyst at MoffettNathanson, speaking directly to the camera from a home setting. Wearing a dark sleeveless top, pearl necklace, and with blonde bobbed hair, she gestures expressively with her red-polished hands as she discusses how issuing and managing card programs are becoming more digitized. She explains that as technology advances, open-loop card programs can better replicate American Express's unique capabilities, allowing for better analytics to understand consumer spending and tailor rewards. Behind her, 'svb Securities' and 'MoffettNathanson' logos are visible, along with a shelf holding a framed photo and blue mugs. The scene then transitions to a close-up of a smartphone displaying a 'PAYMENT APPROVED' message with a green checkmark, followed by a person using a phone and credit card to manage bills at a wooden desk cluttered with papers and a laptop. Next, a man in a suit is shown at a hotel reception, handing a card to a female attendant who processes the payment on a terminal. During these visuals, a narrator's voice explains that despite increased loan loss provisions following a period of high inflation, experts believe Amex is ready to weather a possible recession. Dominick Gabriele, Executive Director and Senior Analyst at Oppenheimer & Co., then appears on a MacBook Pro screen from a well-lit room with a window behind him. Wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and black-rimmed glasses, he speaks during a video call, asserting that while American Express is not immune to a downturn, their focus on high-spending, affluent customers means their credit losses are likely significantly lower than peers targeting average or subprime borrowers. He concludes there's no reason to doubt Amex's strong customer base after the next recession. The segment concludes with a CNBC graphic previewing 'How credit scores work,' featuring a thumbnail of two people discussing finances, as background music fades out.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a CNBC graphic titled 'How credit scores work,' featuring a thumbnail of two people discussing finances, accompanied by gentle, slightly suspenseful background music. The scene transitions to a bustling city street, likely Wall Street, with pedestrians and American flags, as a male narrator begins, "America runs on credit." This is followed by a nighttime city street with traffic and a close-up of a hand swiping a credit card through a payment terminal, while the narrator explains, "The three-digit score represents how likely a person is to pay his or her bills, and it impacts almost every aspect of an American's financial life." Next, Frederick Wherry, Director of Dignity and Debt Network, speaks directly to the camera in a well-lit office, stating, "It's like your passport into everything that you need to do as an adult." Chi Chi Wu, Staff Attorney at the National Consumer Law Center, then appears in a home office with bookshelves, detailing how credit "affects so many things," from credit cards and mortgages to apartments and insurance. An expansive aerial view of a car lot filled with new vehicles and a dramatic low-angle shot of towering city skyscrapers are shown as the narrator warns, "Having a low or no credit score can have severe financial consequences." A close-up of a red credit card leads into a bold graphic highlighting that '42% of Americans were denied a financial product in the past year due to their credit score,' with the narrator emphasizing this statistic. Aaron Klein, Senior Fellow at the Brookings Institution, speaks against an orange background, noting that "life can become more expensive and more difficult as your credit score falls." The segment concludes with a group of three professionals discussing around a laptop in a modern office, as the narrator begins to discuss current credit reporting systems.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a group of three individuals—two men and a woman—engaged in a financial discussion at a sunlit outdoor table, with one man in a maroon blazer gesturing while holding documents, as a male voice states there are "major issues." The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit and checkered shirt speaking directly to the camera in an office setting, asserting that "The current credit scoring system, as it stands, is flawed." This is followed by another man in a gray suit and blue patterned tie speaking against a solid orange background, who adds that "this error of being fundamentally misaligned has huge ramifications across the system for millions of Americans." A narrator then states, "Others say many of these criticisms are misguided," as the narrative shifts to a bank interior, where a woman with a handbag interacts with a teller and a man in a black suit leans over the counter. The video cuts to a series of expert interviews: John Ulzheimer, President of The Ulzheimer Group, is shown in a living room with a fireplace, describing the US credit reporting system as "the crown jewel of the world." Sally Taylor, Vice President and General Manager at FICO, speaks from a room with beige curtains, explaining that "A lot of the criticisms are based on some fundamental misunderstanding of how credit scores are calculated and how they're used." Eli Peterson, Global Deputy General Counsel at TransUnion, appears in a home setting, arguing that credit scores are "actually good for consumers relative to a world without credit scores." A close-up then shows a woman’s hands using a smartphone calculator to compute figures on financial documents, with cash and glasses nearby. The scene returns to the outdoor group, now with the man in the maroon blazer handing papers to the others. A woman with curly hair and glasses is then shown reviewing documents at a kitchen table with a laptop, as the on-screen text 'How credit scores work in America' appears, and a narrator asks, "So how do credit scores work in America? And do they help or hurt consumers?" The segment concludes with a graphic of a credit score meter ranging from 300 to 850, color-coded from red to blue, labeled 'Financial stability,' with the CNBC logo and text 'Credit scores' and 'Broken system? Changes' in the top left corner, accompanied by the start of upbeat, light background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a CNBC graphic titled 'Credit scores', displaying a horizontal bar ranging from 300 to 850, color-coded from red to blue to represent financial stability and creditworthiness, as a male narrator explains that a credit score is a number between 300 and 850 representing financial stability and creditworthiness. The graphic dynamically labels credit score ranges: 'Very bad credit score <580' in red, 'Sub-prime credit score 580-659' in orange, 'Average credit score 716' in green, and 'Elite credit score >720' in blue, sourced from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, while the narrator emphasizes that a higher score indicates a better consumer profile for lenders. The scene then transitions to a clipboard icon symbolizing credit report evaluation, before cutting to John Ulzheimer, President of The Ulzheimer Group. Seated in a living room with a fireplace and floral furniture, wearing a light blue shirt, he speaks directly to the camera with a clear, authoritative voice, gesturing with his hands as he explains that "Credit scores in the 500s are considered to be very bad," and in the 600s, terms like "subprime" or "near prime" are used, indicating they are "getting close to average." He further elaborates, stressing the need to reach the 700s to hit the national average (710-720) and then progress into the "elite level scores" in the 700s and 800s. While John Ulzheimer remains visible, the male narrator's voice returns, clarifying that "Credit scores and reports are two separate things," with reports being statements of credit information and scores being calculations based on that report data. John Ulzheimer then takes over again, using an analogy to explain the difference: "The credit report is like the test you took," and "The credit score is the grade you got on the test," emphasizing that while "one is influential over the other, but they're not the same thing." The video then transitions to a webpage screenshot of FICO® Scores, highlighting its widespread use by 90% of top lenders and impressive statistics like '10 Billion FICO® Scores are purchased every year' and '27 Million FICO® Scores are purchased every day,' reinforcing its industry standard status, as the narrator begins to explain that "Today's scores [are] calculated by FICO..." The CNBC logo remains visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with an overhead shot of a man in a green sweater intensely focused on financial calculations at a desk, surrounded by a laptop, calculator, notepad, and a tablet displaying 'TRAVELER'S CHECKS', as a male narrator explains how streamlined processes have enabled Americans to enjoy low interest rates for many years. The video then transitions to Aaron Klein, Senior Fellow at the Brookings Institution, seen speaking directly to the camera against an orange background, though the voice heard is that of John Ulzheimer, President of The Ulzheimer Group, who explains how the credit scoring system allows industry to quickly and cheaply make credit decisions, thereby lowering costs for lenders and consumers. Next, a young couple, smiling and engaged in conversation with a financial advisor, is shown, while the voice of Aaron Klein now explains that without this system, lenders would mitigate risk through higher interest rates or more declinations. John Ulzheimer is then visually present, speaking from a home setting and gesturing, as Aaron Klein's voice continues to elaborate on the difficulty of booking an applicant likely to default on a loan. The video cuts to an overhead shot of hands sorting financial documents on a wooden desk, followed by a close-up of a pen marking a document and a credit report with a '520' score stamped 'REJECTED', highlighting poor credit consequences. John Ulzheimer reappears on screen, still accompanied by Aaron Klein's voice. The final scenes, narrated by the initial male voice, show a suburban house with a 'SOLD' sign, a couple smiling in a car dealership examining a vehicle with a salesperson, and archival black-and-white footage of a man signing a document, as the narrator discusses how representing creditworthiness with numbers helps prevent lending discrimination.

The scene shifts to hands sorting bills and receipts on a wooden table next to a smartphone displaying a financial app, followed by an aerial shot of a dense urban neighborhood. Finally, a woman with short brown hair, wearing a patterned blazer, speaks from a home setting with a dark door and beige curtains, stating that "we're able to assess credit risk looking only at the past behavior."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Gray Suit, wearing a gray suit with a blue patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera against a solid orange background, gesturing with his hands as he explains how individuals can inherit derogatory credit information or, as new immigrants, lack a credit history, leading to an 'unscored' status which he describes as a 'bad place to be' in credit scoring. The scene transitions to a graphic overlay stating '19% of Americans have no credit history or are considered “unscoreable”' with a source citation to Experian, displayed over a blurred image of a person at a desk, while a distinct narrator's voice reads this statistic aloud. The video then cuts to Frederick Wherry, Director of the Dignity and Debt Network, speaking in a professional office setting with a window and framed certificates in the background, wearing a dark blue blazer over a checkered shirt and glasses. He elaborates on how credit scores amplify inequalities, making already disadvantaged individuals 'doubly disadvantaged' and objectifying them as 'not worthy' of a chance. Concurrently, close-up shots show hands interacting with financial tools, including one person using a smartphone and pen over documents near a laptop, and another, wearing a yellow long-sleeve shirt and a black smartwatch, holding a gold credit card and typing on a keyboard, with warm lighting emphasizing digital financial activity.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a person in a yellow long-sleeve shirt holding a gold credit card while typing on a white keyboard, with a small potted plant in the foreground and a black smartwatch on their wrist, as a male voice discusses 'fines and fees.' The scene transitions to a couple at a desk, reviewing financial documents and using a laptop, while a second male narrator explains that 'Errors in credit reports can also often lead to miscalculated scores,' and a text overlay states '34% found at least one error on their credit reports' from Consumer Reports. This is followed by a graphic from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau's 2021 report, highlighting 'Incorrect information on your report' as the most common complaint among over 700,000 credit or consumer reporting complaints. A man in a gray suit with a blue patterned tie then speaks directly to the camera against a solid orange background, gesturing as he shares a personal experience: 'For me, it was a different person named Aaron Klein who didn't pay their cell phone bill in New Jersey. I also happened to live in New Jersey for graduate school and this stuck with me for years.' Next, a woman with pink nail polish is shown using a stylus on a tablet, as a female voice states that 'One in 20 have been errors so serious that could cost them either the ability to get credit or a job or an apartment.' The scene then shifts to a time-lapse of a crowded city street with pedestrians and yellow taxis, including a visible 'CHASE' sign, while the second male narrator notes, 'However, those within the industry argue otherwise.' Eli Peterson, Global Deputy General Counsel at TransUnion, then appears from a home setting with a framed sky painting behind him, asserting in a calm voice, 'When we look at our data, it is astronomically reliable. So we're we're constantly auditing and evaluating our data. Our regulators are examining us on a regular ongoing basis. So they're looking at all of our systems around data integrity, data reliability.' The video concludes with close-ups of hands using a calculator and a woman in a black blazer with a blue blouse, working at a desk with a laptop and financial documents, emphasizing financial analysis and calculation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a static display of the Fair Credit Reporting Act (15 U.S.C. § 1681), revised September 2018, highlighting a key provision that mandates credit reporting agencies to conduct a free reinvestigation within 30 days of a consumer dispute, as Eli Peterson's voice is heard discussing how they engage with consumers. A narrator then explains that under this Act, credit bureaus are responsible for correcting inaccurate information, but research suggests many consumers find errors difficult to fix. This is visually reinforced by a bar chart, 'CHART 2: Complaints to the CFPB about Credit Reporting Investigations Transmitted to the Credit Bureaus in 2017,' showing that a majority of complaints (59% for Equifax, 64% for TransUnion, 63% for Experian) were that the investigation did not fix errors. Chi Chi Wu, Staff Attorney at the National Consumer Law Center, then appears in her home office, gesturing as she speaks with a clear, articulate voice, likening credit bureaus to a judge who always rules for the defendant, illustrating how even documented proof of a non-late payment might still appear as late. The scene cuts to a close-up of hands using a calculator and pen on financial documents, while Aaron Klein, Senior Fellow at the Brookings Institution, states with a measured, authoritative voice that fixing mistakes is expensive and time-consuming for the industry, which he argues is not incentivized for accuracy, especially if errors are symmetric. Aaron Klein is then seen in a gray suit against a solid orange background, gesturing as he continues to speak. Eli Peterson, Global Deputy General Counsel at TransUnion, then appears in a light blue suit from a home setting with a framed sky painting, explaining in a calm, professional voice that TransUnion's role is to provide reliable, accurate information, without which nobody would engage with them. As Eli Peterson continues to speak about their position, the video transitions to close-ups of a pen marking figures on a financial document and hands using a calculator over a detailed financial statement with stacks of coins and cash in the background, reinforcing the theme of financial scrutiny and dispute resolution.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a light blue blazer and white shirt, speaking directly to the camera from a well-lit, modern home setting, emphasizing the need for incredibly reliable and accurate data and continually engaging with consumers to help them effectively manage their credit and interface about any concerns. The scene then cuts to a close-up of hands meticulously sorting through a thick stack of documents in a black binder, using a red pen and colorful sticky tabs, while a second male voice discusses the critical need for regulation and oversight to ensure fairness and transparency within the industry. The video returns to the man in the blue blazer, who continues to speak, refuting the idea that the industry is unregulated and asserting that it operates in one of the most highly regulated spaces possible. Next, a third distinct male voice is heard as a man with graying hair, wearing a light blue button-down shirt, appears seated in a home office with a fireplace and floral-patterned furniture, gesturing emphatically as he explains the "50-year-old federal statute" known as the Fair Credit Reporting Act, which essentially mandates everything related to credit reports, including consumer rights to challenge information, freeze reports, and obtain copies. As he speaks, a purple graphic displaying the 'Fair Credit Reporting Act' with its legal citation '15 U.S.C § 1681' and 'Revised September 2018' appears, featuring a partial U.S. seal. This is followed by a screen capture of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) website, which the speaker identifies as regulating credit bureaus. The segment concludes with an exterior view of the classical-style Federal Trade Commission Building on Pennsylvania Avenue, NW, which the speaker also mentions as sharing regulatory oversight.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Chi Chi Wu, a Staff Attorney at the National Consumer Law Center, speaking from her home office, wearing a red top and gesturing. She discusses the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, noting their "really good job of trying to reform the credit bureaus," but advocating for "further than more regulation or even more laws." A phone ringing sound briefly accompanies a transition to a screenshot of the CFPB's 'Submit a complaint' page, while a male narrator explains that "credit reporting and scoring agencies have made several changes in response to the criticisms against the industry." This narration continues over a close-up of hands reviewing financial documents with the word 'Changes' on screen, highlighting that "the biggest change comes from the use of alternative data to improve accuracy and inclusion." The scene then shifts to a hand interacting with a tablet displaying financial data as Aaron Klein, Senior Fellow at the Brookings Institution, in a gray suit against an orange background, elaborates on incorporating "non-traditional information." He explains that while paying a mortgage on time increases a credit score, paying rent on time doesn't because "that information isn't reported." The video then shows a man loading a sofa onto a moving truck, followed by a modern brick apartment building, visually representing life changes and housing. Chi Chi Wu's voice returns, explaining how "we've innovated with scores like the FICO Score XD and the UltraFICO Score," which "augment traditional credit data with rich alternative data, such as how you pay your Telco, utilities, as well as information that..." as animated graphics illustrate FICO scores, showing a ladder, then 'FICO SCORE' and 'ultraFICO SCORE' with a gauge and icons representing financial data.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an animated graphic for 'ultraFICO SCORE' on a green background, featuring floating icons of financial documents, credit cards, and calendars. Sally Taylor, Vice President and General Manager at FICO, then appears in a home setting, explaining that the point of these new scores is to allow consumers to find other ways, beyond historical credit, to demonstrate their ability to repay credit, including through their checking and savings accounts. As the U.S. Capitol Building is shown, first during the day and then at dusk, a narrator discusses that more regulatory changes could be coming, highlighting two bills, H.R. 5332 (Protecting Your Credit Score Act) and H.R. 4120 (Comprehensive Credit Act), which were both passed by the House in 2020, aimed at overhauling the credit scoring system with more oversight and provisions to protect consumer credit, though they have yet to be put on a vote by the Senate. A man in a light blue blazer then speaks from a modern home, championing an alternative data piece of legislation called the Credit Access and Inclusion Act, which would encourage the reporting of rental and utility data to bring new data into the system, allowing millions of Americans to access financial products and services. This is visually reinforced by a split-screen showing S. 2417, detailing 'SEC. 2. POSITIVE CREDIT REPORTING PERMITTED' for energy utility and telecommunication firms. The segment concludes with a close-up of hands sorting financial documents on a wooden table, as the narrator states that ultimately, credit in its current state is dependent on the consumer.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a light blue button-down shirt, seated in a home office, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, explaining how to achieve "fantastic credit scores" by never missing a payment and staying out of credit card debt, advising against using them as an income supplement. As he speaks, the scene transitions to a close-up of hands in a gray knitted sweater using a smartphone for internet banking, navigating an app showing a $2,000 balance, with a computer monitor and financial charts in the background. This is followed by a woman in a lavender turtleneck at a desk, holding a credit card while typing on a laptop, and then a retail checkout where a hand inserts a card into a payment terminal for a $2.95 transaction. The man in the blue shirt returns, continuing his financial advice. Subsequently, a woman in a red top, seated in front of a wooden bookshelf, speaks directly to the camera, challenging the idea that managing credit is solely a sign of responsibility. She highlights that many people have negative credit due to "bad luck" like getting sick or losing their job, calling it "crazy" that this would prevent them from getting new credit. The segment concludes with a close-up of a black credit card for 'JOHN SMITH' and a CNBC graphic promoting the next segment: 'How Discover won over the U.S. middle class,' featuring a small video thumbnail of a retail store interior.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, driving background music as a CNBC graphic announces the upcoming segment: 'How Discover won over the U.S. middle class'. The visuals quickly transition, showing a hand inserting a card into a payment terminal, followed by a person in a yellow shirt holding a gold credit card while typing on a keyboard. A time-lapse then captures the bustling activity across multiple levels of the Mall of America, leading to a woman using a laptop with a credit card in hand, suggesting digital transactions. The scene shifts to a large '$100 MILLION' text overlaying a money-counting machine processing U.S. $100 bills. A female narrator begins, explaining that "Credit cards are a trillion dollar industry," with nearly 45 billion swipes in 2018 for products and services worth "just under $4 trillion." She adds that "Americans owe around $1.1 trillion in credit card debt, about $5,700 each." Visually, Roger Hochschild, CEO of Discover, is then shown in a modern office, and his clear, confident male voice takes over, stating, "The US consumer is doing very, very well. Strong consumer sentiment, strong retail spending, very low unemployment. All of those things are great for the credit card industry." The background music continues as a montage of credit card logos, including Visa, Mastercard, American Express, and Chase, appears. The narrator returns, identifying "Giants like Mastercard, Visa, and Amex" as dominating the network market, and "Chase, City, Amex, and Capital One" as the biggest issuers, coinciding with a visual of a Capital One Bank ATM. The segment concludes with the narrator introducing "A quiet but a steady and perhaps lesser talked about competitor is Discover," as a close-up of a Discover Card with 'Cashback Bonus' text is displayed, highlighting its value proposition, before Roger Hochschild's voice is briefly heard again.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Roger Hochschild, CEO of Discover, standing confidently with arms crossed in a modern office, wearing a white shirt and an orange-faced watch, as a microphone icon and audio waveform indicate an interview. He states that Discover is "the number one performing stock of all financials in the S&P for a 10-year period" and "not just an average company." The scene transitions to a bar chart titled 'Worldwide credit card portfolios (2018)', highlighting Discover’s $72.9B portfolio in yellow, as a female narrator explains that Discover has the "10th largest credit card portfolio in the world." Next, a graphic displays the Discover logo with a dynamic number count rising to 57,000,000 cards, with the narrator confirming "57 million Discover cards out there." The video then cuts to Ted Rossman, Industry Analyst from CreditCards.com, speaking against a blue background, who asserts that Discover is "not really for the kind of people that want to fly first class to the Maldives" but "really is kind of for the masses." Following this, Dominick Gabriele, Senior Analyst at Oppenheimer & Co, appears against a red background, explaining that when thinking about the "average consumer," Discover is "right smack in the middle of all these issuers." A bar chart titled 'J.D. Power U.S. credit card satisfaction index (2019)' then appears, with Discover leading at 842, as the narrator confirms that "The Discover credit cards topped the JD Power customer satisfaction survey in 2019." The segment concludes with a nostalgic, sepia-toned family scene overlaid with the title 'How Discover credit cards won over the American middle class', with the narrator posing the question, "So, how did they win over the American middle class?" Upbeat, slightly nostalgic background music begins to play as a close-up shows a hand swiping a Discover card at a payment terminal, with a menu listing 'Network vs. issuer' visible, and the narrator begins to explain that "To understand the credit card industry, it's important to know the difference between a credit card network..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a female narrator's voice explaining financial concepts like "issuer" and "network" over a dynamic black-and-white wireframe animation of a futuristic cityscape, which transitions into a vibrant stock market graph. She clarifies that "Discover and American Express are both an issuer and a network" as a silver Discover Card and a blue American Express sign appear on a split-screen. The scene then shifts to Benji Stawski, a man in a black suit, speaking directly to the camera against a solid blue background, gesturing as he elaborates in a clear, explanatory male voice on how this structure provides "diversity in their business model" and a "stable source of revenue" from the processing side, contrasting it with the less predictable credit side where high interest rates (18-25%) are charged, but also carry risk. As he speaks, a red, animated upward-trending arrow on a blackboard symbolizes growth, followed by hands flipping through financial documents and a time-lapse of a bustling city street. The female narrator then returns, stating that "40% of Americans are transactors" and "60% carry debt from month to month," before introducing Discover CEO Roger Hochschild. Hochschild is first seen seated, then standing in a modern office, with interview indicators. His voice is then heard over the phone, concisely stating, "Our model is lend focused," as a hand swipes a Discover card at a payment terminal, reinforcing the transaction theme.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a person in a white t-shirt holding a child, inserting a card into a payment terminal at a grocery store checkout, with oranges in the background. A male voice, identified as Benji Stawski, explains business models, stating, "We're looking for people and we make most of our money from people who borrow money. American Express's model is much more spend focused." The scene transitions to a bar chart titled 'U.S. credit card portfolios (2018)', highlighting Discover's $72.9B portfolio in yellow, while a female narrator details how "American Express and JP Morgan interchanged the top two slots on outstanding debt... Discover is sixth." Upbeat, slightly retro electronic music then begins as archival footage of a Sears store appears, showing shoppers with carts and a child in a stroller, overlaid with text labels like 'Network vs. issuer' and 'Sears'. The narrator explains, "The Discover credit card was launched in 1986 by Sears Roebuck, the largest retailer at the time." Benji Stawski, in a black suit against a blue background, adds, "Back then it was part of Dean Witter, which was part of Sears." A dramatic sunrise over mountains with 'DISCOVER' superimposed, followed by a close-up of a Discover card, is accompanied by a vintage song snippet singing, "This is the dawn, the dawn of Discover." Benji Stawski then reiterates, "They talked about the dawn of Discover," and continues, "And they really pioneered two main categories: cashback and no annual fee." The video shows a modern retail checkout where a customer hands over a card, followed by a split-screen showing a Discover card with 'FROM A MEMBER OF THE SEARS FINANCIAL NETWORK' text. The narrator explains, "Sears wanted to expand into financial services and decided to accept only the Sears Discover card at its stores." Benji Stawski concludes, "Many merchants actually viewed them as a threat," as the video ends with a close-up of a Discover card being swiped at a payment terminal, and the music fades out.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of hands holding a glossy Town & Country magazine, as a clear, authoritative male voice (identified as Roger Hochschild, CEO of Discover) explains that "accepting a Discover card meant they were helping their rival Sears," leading to "a lot of hesitation and difficulty for Discover establishing itself." This transitions to a vintage ICL cash register displaying a subtotal of $17.98, evoking a nostalgic retail environment. A black-and-white wide shot of a bustling 1990s-era office filled with employees at bulky CRT monitors then appears, while a female narrator states that "In 1993, Dean Witter Discovering Company became a publicly traded company when it spun off from Sears." The scene shifts to a modern-day exterior shot of a Sears store with a large 'STORE CLOSING' sign, as the narrator continues, "Sears eventually filed for bankruptcy in 2018." A New York Times newspaper headline from February 1997, announcing 'MORGAN STANLEY AND DEAN WITTER AGREE TO MERGE,' is shown as the narrator adds, "In 1997, Dean Witter Discovering Company merged with Morgan Stanley." The narrative moves to the exterior of a Discover Financial Services building, with on-screen text listing key themes: 'Network vs. issuer,' 'Sears,' 'Supreme Court decision,' 'Betting on the middle class,' and 'Future.' The narrator describes the "mid-2000s" as "eventful for Discover," noting that "Mastercard and Visa were established in the industry, and Discover wanted in." An aerial view of the U.S. Supreme Court building appears, emphasizing legal influence, as the narrator explains, "In 2004, the Supreme Court upheld a ruling in Discover's favor," adding that "Discover claimed that Mastercard and Visa had harmed its business by preventing their member banks from issuing credit cards from the Discover network." The video returns to the Discover headquarters, then cuts to a close-up of a hand inserting a card into a payment terminal. An archival male voice, with slightly different audio quality, is heard stating they "did everything they could, including reaching out to merchants to tell them to take..." The segment concludes with a shot of Roger Hochschild, CEO of Discover, standing confidently with arms crossed in a modern office, wearing a white shirt and an orange-faced watch, with a microphone icon and audio waveform indicating an interview. Throughout, a subtle, underlying background music track accompanies the narration and commentary.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a time-lapse of a bustling city street, filled with pedestrians and taxis, as a female narrator discusses US-based customer service and acquiring customers who will revolve a balance monthly, all set to background music. Roger Hochschild, CEO of Discover, then appears in a modern office, confidently stating Discover's "relentless focus on a limited set of businesses," contrasting their consumer focus with other banks' involvement in commercial real estate and small business lending. A data visualization titled 'Customer FICO scores (2019)' then illustrates Discover's customer base, with the narrator explaining that 81% have a FICO score of 660 or higher, while American Express caters to a more affluent base with a higher average FICO, and Capital One serves a larger number of subprime borrowers. Roger Hochschild returns, likening Discover to Toyota and American Express to Mercedes. Following this, Johnny Lastname, in a black suit and purple tie, gestures as he describes the typical Discover customer as likely middle to lower-middle class, potentially a parent, and residing in "Middle America." The segment concludes with archival footage of a woman holding a smiling toddler, followed by an aerial view of a peaceful suburban neighborhood with brick houses and light fog.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Johnny Lastname, a man in a black suit and purple tie against a solid blue background, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He explains that they are not necessarily discussing affluent urban professionals who gravitate towards cards like Amex or Chase. The scene then transitions to a detailed graphic of the J.D. Power U.S. credit card satisfaction index from 2014 to 2019, highlighting Discover's consistent top ranking, with the company's name highlighted in yellow across the years. A female narrator's voice confirms that Discover has been voted number one every year since 2014, except for 2017, according to the J.D. Power customer satisfaction survey. The video then cuts to a different speaker, Johnny Lastname, now in a gray blazer over a black turtleneck, wearing black-rimmed glasses, speaking from an office setting with a modern desk and a blue wall panel. He notes that it's very difficult to grow a business in the mature U.S. market, but Discover is doing a really good job of keeping its customers very satisfied with its value proposition. The scene returns to the first Johnny Lastname in the blue background, who continues his explanation, stating that while airline mile cards get more attention due to 'sexier' redemptions like first class and airport lounges, about two-thirds of credit card rewards chasers actually prefer cashback. A brief B-roll shot follows, showing a person's legs walking briskly through a modern, sunlit airport terminal with reflective floors and large windows, carrying a backpack. The video then returns to Johnny Lastname in the blue background, before transitioning to a bar chart titled 'Discover: Total assets minus total liabilities,' showing a steady increase from 2008 to 2019, with the 2019 bar reaching over $11B, sourced from FactSet. The female narrator's voice returns, stating that Discover's balance sheet reflects the company's improving finances since 2008. The CNBC logo is visible in the top right corner throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Dominick Gabriele, Senior Analyst at Oppenheimer & Co, speaking directly to the camera against a solid red background, dressed in a dark suit and blue patterned tie, gesturing as he analytically explains that investors seek "high capital return" and a "stable but growing business model," with payouts around "70% plus" through dividends and share repurchases. The scene then transitions to Roger Hochschild, CEO of Discover, standing with arms crossed in a modern office, wearing a white button-down shirt, who, with a calm and measured voice, remarks on Discover's "Midwest heritage," stating, "We're not one of those companies that's always out there talking about how great we are." A time-lapse of people moving through a spacious, well-lit mall then appears, followed by a bar graph illustrating Discover's underperformance in cumulative total returns compared to the S&P 500 and competitors over one and five years, as a narrator explains that "the last few years haven't stacked up for the Discover stock." The video then shows a line graph detailing Discover's stock (DFS) dropping 11% against the S&P 500 from January to February 2020, which the narrator highlights as the "most it had done in 10 years" following an earnings call. Finally, a Wall Street Journal article screenshot confirms "Discover Shares Plunge on Worries About Troubled Borrowers," with the narrator adding that the company's "troubled debt restructurings" increased by "nearly 50%," reflecting a significant rise in high-risk customers.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a static medium shot of Roger Hochschild, CEO of Discover, standing confidently with his arms crossed in a modern, brightly lit office environment. He is wearing a crisp white button-down shirt and a distinctive orange-faced watch on his left wrist. The background features blurred geometric glass structures, suggesting a contemporary corporate setting. On-screen graphics identify him with a microphone icon and a dynamic yellow audio waveform, indicating an interview or audio feature, while the CNBC logo is visible in the top right corner. Roger speaks articulately and confidently, stating, "This is day and age, it's hard to know what's going to happen, but I would say, we have a complete business model, we're strong on both sides of the balance sheet, if you think about our lending products, but also our deposit products. So I feel very good about how Discover's positioned." As he concludes his statement, the scene transitions to a clean white background with the CNBC logo centered, and a calm, professional background music begins to play. This is followed by a series of end-credit screens, displaying production credits for producers, editors, and graphics teams. The background shifts to a dark blue, abstract design with faint financial data patterns. On the right side, a vertical yellow line separates the credits from a call-to-action: 'Subscribe' above the CNBC logo and the tagline 'FIRST IN BUSINESS WORLDWIDE.' Below the credits, a prompt reads 'Let us know what you think in the comments below' in bold yellow text, with additional sources listed including The Balance, CreditCards.com, Discover, FactSet, and The Federal Reserve. The segment concludes with this focus on branding and audience engagement as the background music continues.
